environmental.snapStatus: Production banned under the Montreal Protocol (US: 1996)

lubricants.notes: Mineral oil is the historical standard. R-11 is a low-pressure refrigerant historically used in centrifugal chillers.

environmental.snapStatus: Industrial use; not SNAP-listed for HVAC

lubricants.notes: Used primarily in low-temperature industrial refrigeration cascade systems. Critical point at 9.2°C (48.6°F) — supercritical in most building-HVAC ambient ranges.

environmental.snapStatus: Production banned under the Montreal Protocol (US: 1996)

lubricants.notes: Mineral oil was standard in R-12 systems. Industry standard for residential refrigerators and mobile AC for decades before HFC transition.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for use as a substitute for R-123 in centrifugal chillers

lubricants.notes: Designed as a low-GWP replacement for R-123 in low-pressure centrifugal chillers.

environmental.snapStatus: HCFC; US production for new equipment ended 2020; servicing of existing equipment allowed with reclaimed/stockpiled supply

lubricants.notes: Low-pressure refrigerant for centrifugal chillers. Higher toxicity (B1) compared with most HCFCs — proper ventilation is required.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for use as a substitute for R-123 in centrifugal chillers and as a foam blowing agent

lubricants.notes: Low-pressure HCFO with very low GWP and short atmospheric lifetime. Engineered as a long-term R-123 replacement.
Z-isomer of R-1233zd. Less common than the E-isomer; specialty foam blowing agent and low-GWP heat-transfer fluid applications.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for mobile AC; class A2L — mildly flammable

lubricants.notes: Industry standard for mobile AC since the 2010s as a low-GWP R-134a replacement. PAG is the OEM mobile-AC standard; POE used in stationary applications.

applications: Mobile air conditioning (passenger vehicles, most OEMs since 2014)

r-1234ze.chemicalName: 1,3,3,3-Tetrafluoropropene (the E isomer is the default unless Z specified)

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for chillers and commercial refrigeration; class A2L

lubricants.notes: Long-term low-GWP replacement for R-134a in chillers, heat pumps, and commercial refrigeration. Same molecule as the explicit E-isomer page; this slug preserves a legacy URL.

r-1234ze.propertiesSource: CoolProp 7.2.0 + Honeywell Solstice ze technical datasheet

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for chillers and commercial refrigeration; class A2L

lubricants.notes: Explicit E-isomer designation. Long-term low-GWP replacement for R-134a in chillers and heat pumps.

r-1234ze-e.propertiesSource: CoolProp 7.2.0 + Honeywell Solstice ze technical datasheet

environmental.snapStatus: Specialty applications; high-temperature heat pumps

lubricants.notes: Z-isomer with higher boiling point than R-1234ze(E). Used in high-temperature heat pumps and organic Rankine cycle applications.

environmental.snapStatus: HCFC; production phased out

lubricants.notes: Historical use as a blend component in R-22 retrofits (R-409A, R-414B). Standalone use is rare.

environmental.snapStatus: AIM Act phase-down affected; primarily used as a blend component

lubricants.notes: Rarely used standalone. Primary applications are as a blend component (R-404A, R-407C, R-410A, R-507A) and as a clean agent (FM-200/HFC-227ea-class fire suppression in some lists, though HFC-227ea is the more common agent).

applications: Blend component in R-404A, R-407C, R-410A, R-507A

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for industrial refrigeration; class A3 — highly flammable

lubricants.notes: Highly flammable (A3). Used in industrial refrigeration where flammability can be managed. Mineral oil is standard; HC-rated equipment design is mandatory.

applications: Some commercial refrigeration in regions with HC-rated equipment

environmental.snapStatus: Production banned under the Montreal Protocol (US: 1996)

lubricants.notes: Ultra-low temperature refrigerant (NBP -81.4°C). Used historically as the low-stage refrigerant in cascade systems for environmental test chambers and cryogenic applications. Critical point at 28.8°C (83.9°F) — chart is partial.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for chillers and heat pumps; foam blowing applications

lubricants.notes: Very low GWP HFO designed for high-temperature heat pumps, organic Rankine cycle, and foam blowing. Higher NBP than R-1234ze(E).

environmental.snapStatus: AIM Act phase-down affected; SNAP-listed for declining set of applications

lubricants.notes: Mobile AC originally used R-134a with PAG oil; stationary equipment uses POE. POE is hygroscopic — pull deep vacuum before charging.

environmental.snapStatus: Primarily used as a blend component; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: Used almost exclusively as a blend component (R-404A, R-507A). A2L mild flammability; high GWP.

applications: Blend component in R-404A, R-507A, R-427A

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for some applications; class A2 — flammable, restricted use

lubricants.notes: Class A2 (flammable, low burning velocity). Used as an aerosol propellant and as a blend component to lower GWP. Some mobile AC research interest as a low-GWP option.

environmental.snapStatus: Very high GWP; restricted to specialty uses

lubricants.notes: Perfluorocarbon (PFC). Extremely long atmospheric lifetime and high GWP. Used in specialty low-temperature applications and as a fire suppression agent (FE-13).

environmental.snapStatus: HCFC; US production for new equipment ended 2010; virgin production for service ended 2020 (Montreal Protocol)

lubricants.notes: Mineral oil is the historical standard. Alkylbenzene (AB) is compatible. POE is NOT used with R-22 — POE is required only for HFC retrofits.

applications: Existing residential central AC systems (servicing only)

environmental.snapStatus: Specialty uses

lubricants.notes: Specialty HFC used in organic Rankine cycle and high-temperature heat pump applications. Limited commercial availability.

environmental.snapStatus: Fire suppression and specialty applications; high GWP under restriction

lubricants.notes: Primarily a fire suppression agent (FE-36). Limited refrigeration use due to high GWP.

environmental.snapStatus: Approved for organic Rankine cycle and low-pressure chillers; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: Low-pressure HFC for organic Rankine cycle and centrifugal chillers. Class B1 indicates higher toxicity than typical HFCs.

applications: Organic Rankine cycle (geothermal, waste heat recovery)

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for household refrigerators, freezers, and small commercial refrigeration with charge limits; class A3 — highly flammable

lubricants.notes: Highly flammable (A3). EPA charge limits apply: 150 g for household refrigerators, increasing limits for commercial under updated SNAP rules. HC-rated equipment design with sealed systems and leak detection is mandatory.

applications: Household refrigerators and freezers (Europe widely; US increasingly)
Small commercial refrigeration (display cases, ice machines)
Heat pumps (small residential, R-290 monobloc designs growing)

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for residential and light commercial AC; class A2L — mildly flammable, special handling required

lubricants.notes: POE required. A2L mild flammability: low burning velocity, but requires A2L-rated equipment, leak detection, and ventilation per UL 60335-2-40 / ASHRAE 15.

applications: Residential central air conditioning (replacing R-410A in new equipment)

environmental.snapStatus: Primarily foam blowing applications

lubricants.notes: Class A2 (flammable). Primarily used as a foam blowing agent for polyurethane insulation. Limited refrigeration use.

environmental.snapStatus: AIM Act phase-down affected; SNAP delisted for many new equipment categories

lubricants.notes: POE required. The standard low-/medium-temperature commercial refrigeration HFC for two decades; high GWP (~3922) makes it the primary phase-down target in commercial refrigeration.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for some commercial refrigeration; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: POE required. Used as a medium-/low-temperature R-22 retrofit, also as a lower-GWP alternative to R-404A in commercial refrigeration. Significant temperature glide (~5-6 K).

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: POE required. The most common R-22 drop-in retrofit. Substantial temperature glide (~7-8 K) — TXV systems should be retuned and EXV is preferred for new equipment. Closely matches R-22 operating pressures.

applications: Residential and light commercial AC (R-22 retrofit)

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for commercial refrigeration; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: POE required. Designed as a lower-GWP, higher-capacity alternative to R-404A in commercial refrigeration. Significant temperature glide.

environmental.snapStatus: AIM Act phase-down affected; SNAP delisted for new residential AC equipment as of Jan 1, 2025

lubricants.notes: POE required. Near-azeotropic (glide <1°F) so it behaves close to a pure refrigerant. Operating pressures roughly 60% higher than equivalent R-22 systems — gauges, hoses, and recovery equipment must be appropriately rated.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed for R-22 retrofit; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: Designed as a mineral-oil-compatible R-22 retrofit (the hydrocarbon component aids miscibility). One of the few HFC blends that does not require an oil change from R-22 systems.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed for R-22 retrofit; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: R-22 retrofit option. Mineral oil compatibility makes it a low-effort retrofit choice.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed for R-502 / R-22 retrofit (low-temp); AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: Mineral-oil-compatible retrofit for R-502 and R-22 low-temperature systems.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed for R-22 retrofit; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: Mineral-oil-compatible R-22 retrofit. Less common than R-422A or R-422D in practice.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed for R-22 retrofit; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: Mineral-oil-compatible R-22 retrofit. Popular medium-temperature drop-in option for commercial AC where oil change is impractical.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed for R-22 retrofit; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: Marketed as 'simple retrofit' from R-22 — designed to work with any of MO/AB/POE oils.

applications: R-22 retrofit (commercial AC, heat pumps, supermarket refrigeration)

environmental.snapStatus: Listed for R-22 retrofit; AIM Act phase-down affected

lubricants.notes: Hydrocarbon components aid miscibility with mineral oil. Marketed by Honeywell as a 'no oil change' R-22 retrofit.

r-438a.propertiesSource: Honeywell Genetron MO99 datasheet + ASHRAE Standard 34-2022

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for commercial refrigeration; replacement for R-404A and R-22 in medium/low-temp applications

lubricants.notes: POE required. Designed as a lower-GWP replacement for R-404A in commercial refrigeration. Significant glide (~5 K).

r-448a.propertiesSource: Honeywell Solstice N40 datasheet + ASHRAE Standard 34-2022

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for commercial refrigeration; R-404A and R-22 replacement

lubricants.notes: POE required. Lower-GWP R-404A replacement, similar capacity. Significant temperature glide.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable; commercial refrigeration and chillers

lubricants.notes: POE required. Lower-GWP R-134a alternative for medium-temp commercial refrigeration, chillers, and heat pumps. Low glide (~0.5 K).

r-450a.propertiesSource: Chemours Opteon XP10 datasheet + ASHRAE Standard 34-2022

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for transport refrigeration; R-404A replacement

lubricants.notes: POE required. Targeted at transport refrigeration where R-404A pressure/capacity match matters. Lower GWP than R-404A but still significant.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for residential and light commercial AC; class A2L

lubricants.notes: POE required. A2L — mildly flammable. Long-term R-410A replacement with very similar operating pressures (within ~5%), enabling near drop-in compatibility for compatible-rated equipment.

applications: Residential and light commercial AC (R-410A replacement)

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for residential and light commercial AC; class A2L — leading R-410A replacement in US for 2025+ equipment

lubricants.notes: POE required. A2L — mildly flammable. The current leading R-410A replacement in US residential split systems. Similar pressures to R-410A; equipment is rated for A2L handling.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for commercial refrigeration; class A2L; below EU F-Gas 150 threshold

lubricants.notes: POE required. A2L. Very low GWP — meets EU F-Gas <150 threshold for stationary refrigeration. Targeted at low/medium-temp commercial refrigeration.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for commercial refrigeration; class A2L; below EU F-Gas 150 threshold

lubricants.notes: POE required. A2L. Very low GWP — meets EU F-Gas <150 threshold. CO2 component contributes a significant temperature glide (~7-9 K).

environmental.snapStatus: Below EU F-Gas 150 threshold; commercial refrigeration applications

lubricants.notes: POE required. A2L. Very low GWP. Less commonly deployed than R-454C or R-455A but offers similar performance envelope.

environmental.snapStatus: CFC blend; production banned under the Montreal Protocol (US: 1996)

lubricants.notes: Azeotropic CFC blend with no glide. Historically used in 60 Hz markets as a higher-capacity alternative to R-12 in 50 Hz applications. Banned for production.

environmental.snapStatus: Production banned under the Montreal Protocol (R-115 component is a CFC)

lubricants.notes: Azeotrope with no glide. Historically the dominant low-temperature commercial refrigerant for supermarkets. Banned because R-115 is a CFC.

environmental.snapStatus: Production banned under the Montreal Protocol (R-13 is a CFC)

lubricants.notes: Azeotrope. Historically used as the low-stage refrigerant in cascade systems for environmental test chambers and cryogenic applications. Banned for production.

environmental.snapStatus: AIM Act phase-down affected; SNAP delisted for many new commercial refrigeration categories

lubricants.notes: POE required. Azeotrope — no glide. Used in low-temperature commercial refrigeration as an alternative to R-404A. Higher GWP than R-404A; primary phase-down target.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for chillers, commercial refrigeration, and heat pumps

lubricants.notes: POE required. Near-azeotropic (low glide). Lower-GWP R-134a replacement for chillers and medium-temp refrigeration.

applications: R-134a retrofit (drop-in compatible in many systems)

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for low-pressure centrifugal chillers; replacement for R-123

lubricants.notes: Very low GWP centrifugal chiller refrigerant. B1 safety class (higher toxicity than typical HFOs) — ventilation and leak detection required.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for centrifugal chillers and medium-temp commercial refrigeration

lubricants.notes: Near-azeotropic. A1 (non-flammable) — unlike pure R-1234ze(E) which is A2L. R-227ea addition shifts the blend to A1 classification.

applications: R-134a / R-1234ze retrofit where A1 is required

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for centrifugal chillers

lubricants.notes: Azeotrope. A1 classification (non-flammable). Lower GWP than R-515A. R-227ea content stays just above the minimum needed to maintain A1 classification.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for some commercial refrigeration; below EU F-Gas 150 threshold

lubricants.notes: POE required. A2L — mildly flammable. Low GWP (under 150). R-134a retrofit option in markets prioritizing low GWP.

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for household refrigeration and small commercial; class A3 — highly flammable

lubricants.notes: Highly flammable (A3). Mineral oil standard. EPA charge limit 150 g for household refrigerators; updated SNAP rules expanded to small commercial applications.

applications: Wine coolers and small beverage merchandisers

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable for industrial refrigeration; class B2L — toxic and mildly flammable

lubricants.notes: Class B2L — TOXIC and mildly flammable. Workplace exposure limit (NIOSH IDLH 300 ppm; OSHA TWA 50 ppm). Industrial refrigeration standard for decades; charge handling, leak detection, and ventilation are strict regulatory requirements. Ammonia attacks copper — use steel piping and components.

applications: Industrial refrigeration (food processing, cold storage warehouses)
verifiedAgainst: IIAR (International Institute of Ammonia Refrigeration) standards
r-717.gwpSource: IPCC AR5 Table 8.A.1 (ammonia is not a GHG)

environmental.snapStatus: Listed acceptable broadly; the GWP=1 reference refrigerant

lubricants.notes: Critical point at 31.0°C (87.8°F) — most commercial systems operate transcritically. Very high operating pressures (typically 60-130 bar). Specialty equipment, POE oil (specific CO2-rated formulations), and high-pressure components required.

applications: Commercial refrigeration (supermarket booster and transcritical CO2 systems, expanding rapidly)
Heat pumps (water heating, with high COP at moderate ambient)
r-744.gwpSource: IPCC AR5 (CO2 is the GWP reference, defined as 1)

environmental.snapStatus: Specialty applications; very high GWP and atmospheric lifetime

lubricants.notes: Perfluorocarbon (PFC) — extremely persistent in the atmosphere. Specialty uses; food propellant (FDA approved), semiconductor etching, electronics dielectric. Very limited refrigeration use.
